author: @mikeg4972
text:
Great video, thanks for posting!
Gas shielding would be great for protecting the laser optics.
I want to put together my own gas shielding using some old Mig welder parts.
If I'm successful., I'll post it on my Youtube channel.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Great to know. My mind immediately went to a fan fitted to blow across the burning area to prevent smoke building up in the first place, maybe even slightly cooling the colet as a secondary effect.

author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
text: lens cracking under stress? or maybe sb tried to focus the lens beyond limits and broke it

author: @jwioo
text: Cheers Rob that's a handy tip.

author: @scotttovey
text:
You can add a blower to the laser to reduce the amount of smoke that gets on the lens. Typically, it's using a small fish tank air pump and running a tube so that the air blows at the part being burned.

Some say it also enables the laser to cut thicker material.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: so my question is have you tested and tried multi layering copper after being lased on one layer of the copper nanoparticles you were making in the very first video I watched of yours XD (ie using it to sort of 3D print copper by testing if the next layer will fuse to the bottom layer below it, in what is normally called 2.5D because the next layer is not automated completely)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: I am also guessing that using a lower power will give a more foam like fused copper so electrodes for batteries could be made, and switching metals will allow further exploration on your battery front :)

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Nice , makes me wonder if you could use a desktop fan or pc fan to avoid some of the problem.

author: @daleguenther5826
text: I really enjoy your videos! Straight forward thinking about possibilities of a problem, and then how to approach correcting the problem. Keep up the great work!

author: @William_Hada
text: Thanks for the great useful tip! That lens does get exposed to a pretty harsh treatment of heat, flame and smoke. Not surprising that they can eventually fail when you think about it.

author: @SlyerFox666
text: Nice tip, not sure if this is an idea you might have already done but have you ever tried making a simple PCB with the laser and a copper clad board before ?
